title: “So too, monks, for one who wanders outside his own proper range, into the domain of others, Māra gains an opportunity, Māra gains an object.…
content: “So too, monks, for one who wanders outside his own proper range, into the domain of others, Māra gains an opportunity, Māra gains an object. And what, monks, is not a monk’s proper range, but the domain of others? It is the five strands of sensual pleasure. What five? Forms cognizable by the eye that are desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. Sounds … odors … tastes … touches cognizable by the body … desirable … enticing. This, monks, is not a monk’s proper range, but the domain of others. Monks, wander in your own proper range, your ancestral territory. When you wander in your own proper range, your ancestral territory, Māra does not gain an opportunity, does not gain an object. And what, monks, is a monk’s proper range, his ancestral territory? It is the four foundations of mindfulness. What four? Here, monks, a monk dwells contemplating the body as a body, ardent, clearly comprehending, mindful, having removed covetousness and sorrow in the world; dwells contemplating feelings as feelings … mind as mind … phenomena as phenomena, ardent, clearly comprehending, mindful, having removed covetousness and sorrow in the world. This, monks, is a monk’s own proper range, his ancestral territory.”

title: “Monks, for one whose greed is not abandoned, whose hatred is not abandoned, whose delusion is not abandoned — he is called one who is…
content:
“Monks, for one whose greed is not abandoned, whose hatred is not abandoned, whose delusion is not abandoned — he is called one who is bound by Māra, trapped in Māra’s snare, subject to the Evil One’s will.

But, monks, for one whose greed is abandoned, whose hatred is abandoned, whose delusion is abandoned — he is called one not bound by Māra, freed from Māra’s snare, not subject to the Evil One’s will.

One who has abandoned greed, hatred, and delusion,
Him they call one with a developed mind,
One who has become Brahmā, the Tathāgata, the Buddha,
The one who has overcome enmity and fear,
The one who has abandoned all — such a one is called thus.”

title: “Rādha, whatever is suffering — there you should abandon your desire. And what, Rādha, is suffering? The eye, Rādha, is suffering — there you should…
content: “Rādha, whatever is suffering — there you should abandon your desire. And what, Rādha, is suffering? The eye, Rādha, is suffering — there you should abandon your desire. Forms … Eye-consciousness … Eye-contact … Whatever feeling arises dependent on eye-contact … that feeling is suffering. There you should abandon your desire. The ear … The nose … The tongue … The body … The mind … Phenomena … Mind-consciousness … Mind-contact … Whatever feeling arises dependent on mind-contact … that feeling is suffering. There you should abandon your desire. Whatever is suffering, Rādha — there you should abandon your desire.”

title: “I do not see any other thing, monks, through which unarisen unwholesome states arise and arisen wholesome states decline, as lack of clear comprehension. For…
content: “I do not see any other thing, monks, through which unarisen unwholesome states arise and arisen wholesome states decline, as lack of clear comprehension. For one lacking clear comprehension, monks, unarisen unwholesome states arise and arisen wholesome states decline.”

title: “Form, monks, is suffering. Whatever cause and condition for the arising of form — that too is suffering. Form that has arisen from suffering, monks,…
content: “Form, monks, is suffering. Whatever cause and condition for the arising of form — that too is suffering. Form that has arisen from suffering, monks, how could it be happiness? Feeling … Perception … Formations … Consciousness is suffering. Whatever cause and condition for the arising of consciousness — that too is suffering. Consciousness that has arisen from suffering, monks, how could it be happiness? Seeing thus, monks, a well-taught noble disciple becomes disenchanted with form … disenchanted with feeling … disenchanted with perception … disenchanted with formations … disenchanted with consciousness. Being disenchanted, he becomes dispassionate … He knows: ‘… there is no more of this state of being.’”

title: “Monks, what one intends, what one plans, and what one has a tendency towards — that becomes a basis for the establishment of consciousness. When…
content: “Monks, what one intends, what one plans, and what one has a tendency towards — that becomes a basis for the establishment of consciousness. When there is a basis, there is an establishment of consciousness. When consciousness is established and grows, there is the descent of name-and-form. With name-and-form as condition, the six sense-bases; with the six sense-bases as condition, contact; with contact as condition, feeling … craving … clinging … existence … birth; aging, death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief, and despair arise. Such is the origin of this whole mass of suffering.”

title: “Monks, for an untaught ordinary person, contacted by feeling born of contact with ignorance, craving arises. From that craving, volitional formation arises. Thus, monks, that…
content: “Monks, for an untaught ordinary person, contacted by feeling born of contact with ignorance, craving arises. From that craving, volitional formation arises. Thus, monks, that formation is impermanent, conditioned, dependently arisen. That craving is impermanent, conditioned, dependently arisen. That feeling is impermanent, conditioned, dependently arisen. That contact is impermanent, conditioned, dependently arisen. That ignorance is impermanent, conditioned, dependently arisen. Monks, knowing thus, seeing thus, the defilements are immediately destroyed.”

title: “Monks, the defilements of gold — because of which gold becomes defiled, not malleable, not wieldy, not radiant, brittle, and not properly workable — likewise,…
content: “Monks, the defilements of gold — because of which gold becomes defiled, not malleable, not wieldy, not radiant, brittle, and not properly workable — likewise, monks, there are these five defilements of the mind because of which the mind becomes defiled, not malleable, not wieldy, not radiant, brittle, and not properly concentrated for the destruction of the defilements. What five? Sensual desire, monks, is a defilement of the mind. Because of this defilement, the mind becomes defiled, not malleable, not wieldy, not radiant, brittle, and not properly concentrated for the destruction of the defilements. Ill-will … sloth and torpor … restlessness and remorse … doubt are defilements of the mind. These, monks, are the five defilements of the mind. Because of these defilements, the mind becomes not malleable, not wieldy, not radiant, brittle, and not properly concentrated for the destruction of the defilements.”

title: “Householders, if those wanderers of other sects ask you: ‘By what basis, by what tradition, do you speak of those venerable ones as follows: Surely…
content: “Householders, if those wanderers of other sects ask you: ‘By what basis, by what tradition, do you speak of those venerable ones as follows: Surely those venerable ones are free from lust, or are on the path to subduing lust; free from ill-will, or on the path to subduing ill-will; free from delusion, or on the path to subduing delusion?’—when asked thus, householders, you should answer those wanderers of other sects: ‘Those venerable ones dwell in remote lodgings in isolated forests. But in such places there are no forms cognizable by the eye that they could see and then delight in; no sounds cognizable by the ear … no odors cognizable by the nose … no tastes cognizable by the tongue … no tangibles cognizable by the body that they could feel and then delight in. Good sirs, by this basis, by this tradition, we speak of those venerable ones as follows: Surely those venerable ones are free from lust, or are on the path to subduing lust; free from ill-will … free from delusion, or on the path to subduing delusion.’”

title: “Here, Mahānāma, a noble disciple dwells with aroused energy, abandoning unwholesome states and acquiring wholesome states, steadfast, vigorous, not shirking the burden of wholesome states.…
content: “Here, Mahānāma, a noble disciple dwells with aroused energy, abandoning unwholesome states and acquiring wholesome states, steadfast, vigorous, not shirking the burden of wholesome states. He has mindfulness, endowed with supreme mindfulness and discernment, recollecting and recalling what was done and said long ago. He has wisdom, endowed with wisdom regarding arising and passing away, noble and penetrating, leading to the complete destruction of suffering. Thus, Mahānāma, a noble disciple is endowed with noble qualities.”
